children.value: Having a strict lifestyle, such as focusing more on studying and reducing leisure activities, may improve students’ academic performance.
Students spending more time studying tend to achieve higher academic performance.

children.value: Four datasets show the same patterns, in which students in the high grade group tend to spend more time studying than students in the medium and low grease group. In the dataset Student Alcohol Consumption, students who study Math will have higher study time than those who study Portuguese. Overall, even though the datasets come from different samples and use different grading systems, the consistent pattern indicates that academic performance is strongly correlated with higher academic performance.

For this sub-argument, we used bar charts to show the comparison of the average study time across low, medium, and high grade groups. The length of the bar and the position encode the average study time, helping the audience have a clear insight about the comparison. In the Student Alcohol Consumption dataset, we also used multiple charts with different color coding for Math and Portuguese to compare how the study time in different subjects affects their final grade. This approach allows us to indicate the consistent pattern across datasets while avoiding misleading comparison between different grading systems and measurement scales.
Student with better academic discipline tend to perform better in academic performance.

children.value: The dataset gaming and student habit, which uses attendance percentage, show that students in high grade groups tend to have slightly higher average attendance and more academic discipline. The alcohol consumption dataset, which uses the opposite measurement - absence level, shows that students who achieve higher grades consistently have fewest average absences, while those in low and medium grade groups tend to be absent more often. Moreover, students studying Math tend to be absent more than students studying Portuguese.

For this substantiation, we used bar charts as academic percentage and absence level are numerical variables. The x-axis indicates students grade group, while y-axis shows the average attendance or absence level. In the Student Alcohol Consumption dataset, Math students are shown in different coding color with Portuguese students. Color is used as categorical encoding, helping audience distinguish whether the absence pattern differs between two subjects. This allows audience to see the comparison whether students with higher academic performance also show stronger academic discipline (high attendance and low absence).
Students who get higher grades tend to combine intensive study time with extra classes

children.value: In this substantiation, the line chart was used to show the academic investment represented by paid extra classes. The visualization indicates that the relationship between extra classes and study time lead to higher grades of students. Students who combine intensive study time with additional classes often achieve higher grades. However, the pattern does not show the same regarding Math and Portuguese. When it comes to Math students, those who joined academic support classes tend to have gradually increased grades even if they have low or high study time. In contrast, Math students who do not attend extra classes will have lower grades if they spend less time studying, but higher grades when they invest enough time for studying. Therefore, whether math students attend extra classes or spend more time studying may not fully explain their grades; their performance may also depend on problem-solving skills, learning strategies, and conceptual understanding (

). In terms of Portuguese, students who spend more time studying without extra classes tend to achieve higher grades than those who combine both of them. This difference lies in the nature of the subject. Mathematics requires teaching and various learning methods from the teacher (
), while with foreign languages, self-study and practice will be more effective in improving students’ language skills and vocabulary (

The line charts are used to describe the relationship between combined study time and extra classes and average final grade of students. For both charts, the final grade is interval variable (y-axis), while study time is ordinal (x-axis). The two colored lines show students who attended extra classes and those who did not attend, coding as the legend of the graph. This design is useful because it shows the difference between extra-class groups, and the patterns change across different levels of study time.
Despite may affect mental wellbeing, strictly follow intense study schedule brings fruitful result

GPA by Lifestyle and Stress Level"},"xaxis":{"title":{"text":"Lifestyle"}},"yaxis":{"range":[0,4],"title":{"text":"Average GPA"}}}}

children.value: Strictly living students experience moderate to high stress, but also achieve higher GPA, specifically a 0.2 gap for high stress and 0.1 gap for moderate
Because GPA has the maximum of 4 and is the average of 100+ ECTs, a difference of 0.1 is already notable

The clustered columns chart is chosen to illustrate data for the argument because there are 2 main variables: average GPA (ratio)and stress level (categorical), which is grouped into 2 groups of “Strict” and “Chill”. This type of chart helps show the correlation of grade and stress for each group. Additionally, aligning the 2 groups side-by-side assist comparison. Different shades of red are used to represent stress levels, bringing the necessary contrast for readers to easily notice levels of stress: the more intense the red shade, the higher the stress level.
